subject: Physics
solution:
The data given is 

l=distance between the charges

q=charge

m=mass of the positive charge

2m=mass of negative charge 

The moment of inertia of the system is 

I=m×2mm+2ml2=2ml23

The angular frequency can be written as 

ω=pEI   ...(i)

Substituting the value of moment of inertia in the equation (i) we get 

ω=pE2ml23=3pE2ml2

The value of the dipole moment is

p=ql

Hence, the angular frequency becomes

ω=3qE2ml

This question was given bonus by NTA as none of the options matched in the original paper.

subject: Physics
solution:
Net electrostatic energy for the system $\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{K}\left[\frac{\mathrm{q}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{\mathrm{Qq}}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{\mathrm{Qq}}{\mathrm{a} \sqrt{2}}\right]=0$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow q &=-Q\left[1+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow Q=\frac{-q \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}+1} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Charge and charge density on both isolated metallic solid spheres are show below.

Let the charges and the charge densities on both the spheres after connected by thin conducting wire be Q1', Q2', σ and σ' as shown below.

Once connected, charge on both the spheres will redistribute such that potentials at the surface of both spheres is the same.

∴kQ1'R=kQ2'2R

⇒Q1'4πε0R=Q2'4πε02R

∴Q2'=2Q1'

Using charge conservation, 

Q1'+Q2'=Q1+Q2

∴Q2'2+Q2'=20πR2σ

32Q2'=20πR2σ

∴Q2'4π2R2=23×20πR2σ16πR2

∴σ'σ=56

subject: Physics
solution:
The charged bob experiences two forces at equilibrium:
Gravitational force: $F_g = mg$ (downward)
Electric force: $F_E = qE$ (horizontal, in direction of field)
These forces are perpendicular to each other. The string tension must balance their resultant.
At equilibrium, the string makes angle $\theta$ with vertical such that:
Vertical: $T\cos\theta = mg$
Horizontal: $T\sin\theta = qE$
The net force from gravity and electric field is $\sqrt{(mg)^2 + (qE)^2}$
Therefore, the tension in the string is: $T = \sqrt{m^2g^2 + q^2E^2}$

$$

subject: Physics
options: Net potential of the system cannot be zero at a point but net electric field can be zero at that point
Net potential of the system at a point can be zero but net electric field can't be zero at that point
Both the net potential and the net field can be zero at a point
Both the net potential and the net electric field cannot be zero at a point
solution:
As we know, net potential will be algebraic sum of potential due to each charge, so we can write Vnet=∑kQiri.

As given in the question, Qi and ri are positive, so

∴  Vnet>0

But electric field is a vector quantity, so it can be zero for a given arrangement of charges. For example, electric field at the centre of the two equal charges at certain distance will be zero as electric field due to both charges will be in opposite direction and hence will cancel each other.

subject: Physics
solution:
The force body diagram $F_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_3}{a^2}$ $\mathrm{F}_2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{q}_1 \mathrm{q}_3}{\mathrm{~b}^2}$
$\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{X}}=\mathrm{F}_1 \sin \theta+\mathrm{F}_2=\frac{\mathrm{q}_1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{\mathrm{q}_3}{\mathrm{a}^2} \sin \theta+\frac{\mathrm{q}_1}{\mathrm{~b}_2}\right] \Rightarrow \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{X}} \propto\left(\frac{\mathrm{q}_3}{\mathrm{a}^2} \sin \theta+\frac{\mathrm{q}_2}{\mathrm{~b}^2}\right)$

subject: Physics
solution:
Let the distance of point $x$ from the origin $O$ be $r$.

Point $x$ lies on the axial line of dipole A. The electric field at $x$ due to dipole A is directed along the horizontal axis and its magnitude is given by:
$E_A = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{2p_1}{r^3}$

Point $x$ lies on the equatorial line of dipole B. The electric field at $x$ due to dipole B is directed perpendicular to the horizontal axis and its magnitude is given by:
$E_B = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{p_2}{r^3}$

The resultant electric field makes an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the line $Ox$ (the horizontal axis). Therefore, the tangent of this angle is the ratio of the perpendicular component of the electric field to the parallel component:
$\tan 60^{\circ} = \dfrac{E_B}{E_A}$

Substituting the expressions for $E_A$ and $E_B$:
$\sqrt{3} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{p_2}{r^3}}{\dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{2p_1}{r^3}}$

$\sqrt{3} = \dfrac{p_2}{2p_1}$

$\dfrac{p_2}{p_1} = 2\sqrt{3}$

Answer: $2\sqrt{3}$

subject: Physics
solution:
According to Gauss's law, the electric flux through a closed surface is given by $\Phi = \dfrac{q_{\text{enclosed}}}{\varepsilon_0}$.

For the first sphere of radius $r_1 = 3$ cm centered at the origin, only the charge at $x = 2$ cm is enclosed.

$q_{\text{enclosed}, 1} = 8 \, \mu\text{C}$

$\Phi_1 = \dfrac{8 \, \mu\text{C}}{\varepsilon_0}$

For the second sphere of radius $r_2 = 5$ cm centered at the origin, both charges at $x = 2$ cm and $x = 4$ cm are enclosed.

$q_{\text{enclosed}, 2} = 8 \, \mu\text{C} - 2 \, \mu\text{C} = 6 \, \mu\text{C}$

$\Phi_2 = \dfrac{6 \, \mu\text{C}}{\varepsilon_0}$

The ratio of the electric flux is:

$\dfrac{\Phi_1}{\Phi_2} = \dfrac{8}{6} = \dfrac{4}{3}$

Answer: $4 : 3$

subject: Physics
solution:
From $V = ar^3 + b$, the electric field is $E = -dV/dr = -3ar^2$.
By Gauss's law:
$E(r) \cdot 4\pi r^2 = Q(r)/\epsilon_0$, so $-3ar^2 \times 4\pi r^2 = Q(r)/\epsilon_0$.
This gives $Q(r) = -12\pi ar^4\epsilon_0$.
At $r = 1$: $Q(1) = -12\pi a\epsilon_0$.
Since $Q(1) = \alpha \times \pi a\epsilon_0$, we have $\alpha = -12$

solution:
The distance between q and Q is r=x2+d22=x2+d24.

The force between q and Q will be F=kqQr2.

From the given figure we can see that only vertical components of the forces will add, and the horizontal components will get cancelled out. Therefore,

Fnet=2kqQr2cosθ=2kqQr2×xr=kqQxx2+d2432

For force to be maximum, dFnetdx=0.

⇒kqQx2+d2432-x×32x2+d2412×2xx2+d24322=0

⇒x2+d24=3x2⇒2x2=d24⇒x=d22
